front: What is a multilevel hierarchy in Java?
back: A multilevel hierarchy is an inheritance structure where a class is derived from a base class, and then another class is derived from that derived class, forming a 'chain' of inheritance (e.g., Class A -> Class B -> Class C).

front: In the hierarchy Animal -> Mammal -> Dog, which class is the superclass of the Dog class?
back: In this multilevel hierarchy, Mammal is the direct superclass (parent) of the Dog class. Animal is also a superclass, but it is an indirect superclass (grandparent).

front: How does constructor calling work in a multilevel hierarchy when an object of the bottom-most class is created?
back: Constructors are called in order from the top of the hierarchy down to the bottom. The constructor of the root superclass executes first, followed by the next subclass, and so on, until the constructor of the instantiated class itself finishes last.

front: What is the key advantage of using a multilevel hierarchy over a single level of inheritance?
back: It promotes greater code reusability. Each level in the hierarchy can add a specific, layered set of features. Common attributes and behaviors are defined at high levels and are inherited by all subclasses, while more specific details are added at lower levels.

front: In Java, can a class directly extend multiple superclasses to create a hierarchy? Why or why not?
back: No, Java does not support multiple inheritance for classes. A class can only directly extend one superclass. However, a multilevel hierarchy is built by each class extending exactly one other class, forming a linear chain.
